operator_context.workflow: one window per repo/working directory; tabs within each window for agents and shell
operator_context.pain_point: no global/cross-window view of agent state; tab-hopping does not scale as parallel agent count and repo count grow

items.title: Add coordinator SSE feed aggregating agent, lock, work, worktree, and approval state
items.problem_statement: Coordinator has ~40 HTTP routes exposing agents (discovery.py), locks, work queue, audit, handoffs, approvals — but every consumer must poll. There is no push feed. Worktree registry (.git-worktrees/.registry.json) is file-local per repo and invisible to central views. A single Server-Sent Events endpoint (/events/stream) that multiplexes registration, heartbeat, lock acquire/release, work claim/complete, approval pending, and guardrail block events would unlock every downstream UI — TUI, web dashboard, Tauri shell, menu bar — without bespoke backends.
items.recommended_action: /plan-feature 'Add coordinator SSE event stream aggregating agent/lock/work/approval state for live observers'
items.why_now: Prerequisite for any visualization surface. Event bus infrastructure (event_bus.py) already exists. Enables TUI and dashboard work to proceed in parallel after landing.
items.notes: Complements harness-engineering-features Feature 7 (throughput dashboard), which is currently report-only.

items.title: Aggregate per-repo worktree registries into a coordinator-side index
items.problem_statement: Each repo keeps its own .git-worktrees/.registry.json with owner/branch/heartbeat/pin. Running multiple repos (your workflow) means the operator cannot see all active worktrees in one place. A lightweight agent-side reporter that POSTs registry deltas to the coordinator on heartbeat would create a single queryable index of 'where is every worktree in every repo right now?'
items.recommended_action: Combine with add-agent-state-stream into one change
items.why_now: Your multi-repo workflow needs cross-repo visibility. worktree.py already writes the registry; teaching it to mirror to the coordinator is a ~50-line addition.

items.title: Textual-based TUI showing global agent, lock, work, and worktree state
items.problem_statement: Given your ghostty workflow (window-per-repo, tabs-per-agent), a TUI that you launch in its own tab or window gives a global view that is native to the terminal and requires zero installation beyond 'uv run'. Python/Textual matches the existing skills stack. Panels: active agents with heartbeat, current locks with holders, pending approvals, work queue depth, merge-train status, per-repo worktree list. Subscribes to the SSE stream for live updates.
items.recommended_action: /plan-feature 'Add Textual TUI for global agent state observation' after stream lands
items.why_now: Fits your existing ghostty workflow perfectly: it's just another tab. No new language stack. No packaging. Python-native so skills/.venv hosts it. Proves the data model and usage patterns cheaply before any desktop/web app investment.

items.title: Coordinator-hosted web dashboard (HTML+JS served by FastAPI)
items.problem_statement: A browser-based dashboard at coord.rotkohl.ai/dashboard that shows the same views as the TUI but with richer charts (lock contention over time, task throughput, review convergence timelines) by pulling from OTEL/Prometheus via the existing langfuse_middleware/telemetry modules. Zero new runtimes: served as static files by the existing FastAPI app. Benefits: pin-able tab, shareable URLs to specific views, works on mobile when you want a glance from phone.
items.recommended_action: defer until TUI validates the data model and the most-used views
items.why_now: Natural follow-on to the SSE stream. Benefits from your Cloudflare tunnel work (access from anywhere). Can be iterated cheaply once TUI proves which panels matter.

items.title: Provisioned Grafana dashboards over existing OTEL/Prometheus metrics
items.problem_statement: The coordinator already emits lock.contention, queue.wait_time_ms, queue.task.duration_ms, and policy metrics. Grafana dashboards against these give you historical analytics (throughput, contention hotspots, per-agent-type failure rates) for effectively zero code — just provisioning YAML. Fills the 'trends and post-mortem' view that real-time UIs (TUI/web) don't serve well.
items.recommended_action: Can proceed independently; great as a parallel package once Prometheus exporter is wired up
items.why_now: Highest leverage-per-line-of-code. OTEL is already there; Grafana just renders it. Complements (does not compete with) TUI/web dashboard — different time horizons.

items.title: Tauri desktop shell wrapping the web dashboard with tray/notifications
items.problem_statement: A Tauri (Rust+JS) app that embeds the web dashboard gives system-tray presence, native notifications for 'approval pending' or 'lock contended', and a persistent window decoupled from browser tab management. The user's question was specifically about this form factor (or Swift). Concrete advantages over the web dashboard: a) survives browser restart, b) native OS notifications without Notification API permissions, c) menu-bar/tray icon showing badge count for things needing attention.
items.recommended_action: defer — revisit only if web dashboard proves insufficient on its own
items.why_now: Introduces a Rust/JS toolchain that nothing else in this repo uses. Maintenance cost for a single operator is high. Better to prove value via TUI/web first; Tauri shell is a thin wrapper once the web app exists.

items.title: Native Swift menu-bar status item (macOS only)
items.problem_statement: Swift MenuBarExtra app showing a compact status indicator: count of agents needing input, current open locks, work queue depth. Clicking reveals a popover. Best possible UX for passive glances, but platform-locked (macOS) and introduces a Swift/Xcode stack with zero reuse elsewhere in the repo.
items.recommended_action: defer — reconsider only if macOS remains the single target and passive-glance UX becomes a repeated ask
items.why_now: High UX ceiling but worst strategic fit: platform-locked, new toolchain, no reuse. A Tauri tray icon covers 80% of the value cross-platform.

combined_recommendation: Ship add-agent-state-stream + add-worktree-registry-aggregator + add-agent-state-tui as a single change 'add-agent-state-observability'. This delivers a working global view in your existing ghostty workflow with zero new language stacks, and leaves the web/Tauri/Swift form-factor decisions as reversible follow-ups.
alternative_framing: The user's Tauri-vs-Swift question frames the problem as 'which shell?' The bigger decision is 'what data feed and which views matter?' Answer that with the TUI first; shell choice becomes a 1-2 week follow-up, not a 4-6 week commitment.
